front: Legend: this empire's founder dreamed of a moon entering his chest and a tree growing from his navel that shaded the whole world
Empire: its crescent-and-star flag symbol may have been adopted from the earlier Byzantine Empire
Concept: expansionary myth invoked by contemporary Turkish nationalists to promote irredentism
Empire: founded by Osman I, chief of the Kayı tribe of Oghuz Turks, who settled his people in the region of Bithynia
work: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)

front: Event: this empire's first recorded military victory, won by its mythologized founder
work: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)

front: Concept: Paul Wittek's now-discredited theory attributing the Ottoman Empire's early growth to holy war against Byzantium
work: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)

front: Historian: argued the Ottoman Empire's rise came from tribal organization and assimilation of diverse populations, opposing the Ghaza thesis
work: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)

front: Historian: attacked an older narrative about the Ottoman Empire's early history by calling the period a documentary "black hole"
work: Founding and the Ghaza Thesis Debate (13th-14th century)

front: Event: siege cannon nicknamed the "Basilic," acquired via negotiations with the Hungarian engineer Orban, helped capture Constantinople in 1453
back: Fall of Constantinople / Basilic cannon (Ottoman Empire)
work: Conquest of Constantinople and Mehmed II

front: Ruler: appointed the Christian Patriarch Gennadius II to a position of power after conquering Constantinople in 1453
work: Conquest of Constantinople and Mehmed II

front: Painting: a 1480 portrait of Sultan Mehmed II by this Venetian artist
work: Conquest of Constantinople and Mehmed II

front: Ruler: controversially allied with Francis I of France against their shared Habsburg rivals, jointly besieging Nice in 1543
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Ruler: dubbed "the Lawgiver" domestically, while Westerners called him "the Magnificent"
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Battle: 1538 naval battle in which the Ottoman admiral Hayreddin Barbarossa defeated Andrea Doria, securing Mediterranean naval supremacy
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Battle: 1571 battle that ended the Ottoman Empire's Mediterranean naval dominance
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Event: 1565 Ottoman siege repelled by Jean de La Valette's Knights Hospitaller
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Figure: Ottoman cartographer-admiral who compiled the Kitab-i Bahriye and a celebrated 1513 world map, recapturing Aden but failing to retake Hormuz from Portugal
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Battle: Francisco de Almeida's Portuguese fleet defeated a coalition including Ottoman, Mamluk, and Gujarati forces in the Indian Ocean
work: Suleiman the Magnificent, the Franco-Ottoman Alliance, and Mediterranean/Indian Ocean Naval Power

front: Battle: 1514 battle in which Selim I crushed Shah Ismail I's Qizilbash forces, shattering the Safavid ruler
Ruler: after crushing the Safavids at Chaldiran, this sultan conquered the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517, seizing Mecca and Egypt and taking the title of caliph
Battle: week-long Ottoman-Safavid battle fought continuously by torchlight
Treaty: consolidated the earlier Peace of Amasya between the Ottoman and Safavid Empires
Institution: system that took Christian boys, often from the Balkans, and converted them into Janissary soldiers or administrators (the ablest could rise to Grand Vizier)
Institution: elite Ottoman infantry corps recruited through the devshirme system
Institution: land grants exchanged with sipahi cavalrymen for military service, distributed across sanjaks
Institution: highest-ranking female position in the imperial harem, held by the reigning sultan's mother and guarded by the Chief Black Eunuch
Period: era beginning with Suleiman the Magnificent's marriage to Roxelana, during which royal mothers like Nurbanu and Turhan held outsized power
Institution: 19th-century Ottoman legal code that supplemented earlier regional kanunname codes
Institution: system granting the empire's religious minorities (Christians and Jews) autonomy over their own internal affairs
work: Religious Minorities: Jewish and Christian Communities

front: Figure: Portuguese Jew who became a powerful diplomat and reformer at the Ottoman court
work: Religious Minorities: Jewish and Christian Communities

front: Event: 1840 blood libel accusation supported by French consul Ulysse de Ratti-Menton after the disappearance of Father Thomas
work: Religious Minorities: Jewish and Christian Communities

front: Figure: merchant persecuted by a regional Ottoman governor, prompting him to move to the British Empire and build an opium-trade fortune funding a mansion named Sans Souci
work: Religious Minorities: Jewish and Christian Communities

front: Concept: system confining potential heirs in seclusion rather than executing rivals, cited by Bernard Lewis as a symptom of the Ottoman Empire's decline
work: Decline, the "Sick Man of Europe," and Historiography

front: Concept: 19th-century nickname for the Ottoman Empire, invoked in debates like Gladstone's Midlothian campaign
work: Decline, the "Sick Man of Europe," and Historiography

front: Event: 1711 campaign in which Peter the Great's army was defeated by Ottoman forces under Grand Vizier Baltadji Mehmet Pasha
Treaty: ended the 1768-74 Russo-Turkish War, making the Crimean Khanate a nominally independent Russian satellite and naming Russia protector of the empire's Orthodox Christians
Treaty: ended the 1877-78 Russo-Turkish War, freeing Serbia, Montenegro, and Romania, before being revised by the Treaty of Berlin
Treaty: 1833 agreement with Russia that secretly allowed the Ottoman Empire to close the Dardanelles at Russia's discretion
Document: 1839 decree that began the Tanzimat reforms, promising an end to tax-farming
Event: 1826 violent suppression of the Janissary corps by Sultan Mahmud II
Battle: 1827 naval battle in which a combined British, French, and Russian fleet defeated the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet led by Ibrahim Pasha, securing Greek independence
Event: brutal suppression of an 1876 Bulgarian uprising by bashi-bazouk irregulars, causing international outrage over the "Bulgarian Horrors"
Institution: early 20th-century railway linking Damascus to Medina, later disrupted by T. E. Lawrence during the Arab Revolt
work: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire

front: Event: costly World War I Allied campaign on a peninsula north of the Dardanelles, birthplace of the ANZAC "legend"
work: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire

front: Treaty: secret WWI-era Anglo-French agreement, alongside the Treaty of Sevres, that carved up the Ottoman Empire's Middle Eastern territory
work: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire

front: Treaty: 1923 agreement that replaced the Treaty of Sevres and formally ended the Ottoman Empire
work: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire

front: Concept: title held by Ottoman sultans for 500 years, since Selim I conquered the Mamluk Sultanate, until its 1924 abolition
work: World War I, Partition, and the End of the Empire

front: Novel: Orhan Pamuk novel set among Ottoman miniaturist painters under Sultan Murad III, centering on the murder of the miniaturist Elegant Effendi
Novel: in this Orhan Pamuk novel, the fabric-seller Esther carries love letters between Shekure and Black, whose romance is patterned on Nizami Ganjavi's Khosrau and Shirin
Novel: Ivo Andric novel centered on a bridge with a kapia platform at Visegrad, built through forced labor overseen by Abidaga
back: The Bridge on the Drina (Ivo Andric)

front: Novel: Orhan Pamuk novel in which the scientist Hoja unsuccessfully besieges a fortress with a war machine before escaping the empire's watch
Novel: Ismail Kadare novel following Mark-Alem, who works at a bureaucracy that studies people's dreams, set in a fictionalized version of the empire
Painting: depicts Cossacks laughing while drafting an insult-laden letter of defiance to Sultan Mehmed IV; became the most expensive painting ever sold in Russia after Alexander III bought it
back: The Reply of the Zaporozhian Cossacks (Ilya Repin)
